text: In Letter XLIX, Seneca uses a striking image to describe the pace at which life passes: a spinning top. The faster it turns, the less you can see the individual markings on its surface. He applies this to aging — youth passes so quickly that in retrospect it occupies almost no space at all. But the image cuts deeper: speed creates the illusion of solidity. A life crammed with activity looks full from the outside and feels empty from within. Seneca's prescription is not to slow down but to introduce what he calls mora — deliberate pauses in which you actually register what is happening to you.
question: What illusion does Seneca's spinning-top image identify in a life crammed with activity?

options.text: That speed creates an appearance of fullness while the inner life remains empty
That constant motion gradually destroys one's capacity for wisdom
That youth passes slowly but old age accelerates without warning
That Roman entertainments were designed to distract citizens from virtue
